methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $15M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Housing & Construction (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 1 same-sector peers exist in Housing & Construction, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Inversion Space
private.rationale: Same sector (Housing & Construction), same stage (Series A)

private.name: Cobod
private.rationale: Same sector (Housing & Construction), same stage (Series A)

data.narrative:
Material Evolution, founded by Liz Gilligan and Sam Clark, is a Series A-stage company in Housing & Construction that has raised $15M to date. The comparable universe consists of three private peers (ICON, Inversion Space, Cobod) and zero public comps, limiting the depth of traditional peer benchmarking within the sector.

The analysis relies on stage-progression methodology, which applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A advancement. Using the $15M total raised as a proxy baseline, this yields an implied valuation of $30M. A peer-median approach was not applicable due to insufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables meeting the narrow-filter threshold of three or more datapoints; the single Housing & Construction peer available did not satisfy the required clustering criteria, and a broader sector-wide median would lack meaningful predictive power for this stage and niche.

The triangulation supports a range of $26M to $34M, with a median of $30M. This range reflects primarily the single stage-progression method, with the bounds reflecting reasonable variance around typical Series A-to-Series B step-up dynamics. Key uncertainties include the absence of detailed comparable company metrics (revenue, growth rate, capital efficiency) and the limited depth of the peer set. A more robust valuation would require either additional private company data in the construction-tech subsector or clearer forward revenue and margin guidance from Material Evolution itself.
